response:
module 1: determine the equilibrium population size $ P_e $  
the equilibrium population size $ P_e $ is found by setting $ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0 $, so:  
$ 0 = rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) $  
solving for $ P $:  
$ rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) = h(t) $  
substituting values:  
$ 0.1P\left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) = 10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $  
this is a quadratic equation in $ P $:  
$ 0.1P - 0.0001P^2 = 10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $  
the equilibrium population is the value of $ P $ that satisfies this equation.  

module 2: analyze the stability of the equilibrium population under the new model  
the carrying capacity decreases linearly:  
$ K(t) = 1000 - 3t $  
the equilibrium population under this model is:  
$ P_e(t) = \frac{K(t)}{1 + \frac{r}{K(t)}} $  
substituting values:  
$ P_e(t) = \frac{1000 - 3t}{1 + \frac{0.1}{1000 - 3t}} $  
this function decreases as $ t $ increases, and the population becomes unsustainable when $ P_e(t) \leq 0 $.  

module 3: determine the time $ t $ when the population will no longer be sustainable  
the population is unsustainable when $ P_e(t) \leq 0 $:  
$ \frac{1000 - 3t}{1 + \frac{0.1}{1000 - 3t}} \leq 0 $  
this inequality holds when the numerator is negative:  
$ 1000 - 3t < 0 $  
solving:  
$ t > \frac{1000}{3} $  
$ t > 333.33 $ years
